When I was a young man, I went to teach school in the hills of Tennessee.

I was a student at Fisk University. Every summer, Fisk students went out to find schools in the countryside. We walked the hot dirt roads, asking farmers and families if they needed a teacher. Many doors were closed to us. Many times I heard the words: "Got a teacher. Yes."

I walked for miles in the July heat. I had no horse — horses cost too much. I walked until I found a small, forgotten community in the hills. A girl named Josie told me about a school nearby. She was twenty years old, thin and serious, with quick eyes and a strong voice. She was eager. She wanted to learn.

The schoolhouse was a log cabin that used to store corn. It had no real door. Gaps between the logs served as windows. The benches had no backs, and sometimes no legs. My desk was three boards nailed together. I borrowed a chair from my landlady and returned it each night.

But the children came. They walked down the dusty road in the morning heat, their faces serious and bright at the same time. Josie came with her brothers and sisters. Their family worked a hard rocky farm. The mother was ambitious — she wanted her family to live well. The father was quiet and kind. Josie was the center of everything.

I loved my school. For two summers, I lived in that small world. We read and spelled together. We wrote a little. We picked flowers and listened to stories about the wider world beyond the hills.

Then I left. Ten years passed.

One day I returned to see what had become of my students — to see how life had gone in those years.

Josie was dead.

Her gray-haired mother told me simply: "We've had a heap of trouble since you've been away." Josie had worked every day and night — at home, then in Nashville, saving money for her family's new house. But heartbreak came. One spring, her younger sister shamed the family. Josie grew thin and silent. Then one summer, the person she loved married someone else. She went to her mother like a hurt child, and she slept — and never woke again.

My little log schoolhouse was gone too. In its place stood a new board building — sharper, cleaner, uglier.

I stood there and asked myself: what is progress? Is it this new building — this board and nails — while Josie lies in the ground? How many broken hearts must pay for one step forward? Is the darkness around us the end of the night, or the hour just before dawn?

I did not know the answer. I still do not know. But I carry Josie's face with me, and the question she will never stop asking.
